schools.summary: Gill: the apostle calls the Hebrews 'brethren' not because they were of the same natural stock and lineage but because they were in the same spiritual relation — they all had the same Father, belonged to the same family, were the adopted sons of God, the brethren of Christ, and of one another; and 'holy' because sanctified, and 'partakers of the heavenly calling,' sharers in the call that comes from and leads to heaven; Paul exhorting them to consider the Apostle and High Priest of their profession, Christ Jesus — the summons to fix the mind on Christ in his twofold office (Paul, drawing his conclusion from the dignity of Christ already shown, bidding them 'consider,' fix the attentive eye of faith upon, 'the Apostle and High Priest of our profession,' naming him by two great offices: 'Apostle,' the one sent from God to men to declare his will, answering to and surpassing Moses, and 'High Priest,' the one who goes from men to God, answering to and surpassing Aaron, so Christ unites in himself both offices and is the great object of the believer's contemplation).

themes: wherefore, holy brethren, partakers of the heavenly calling
consider the Apostle and High Priest of our profession
Christ Jesus
Christ uniting the offices of Apostle and High Priest

schools.summary: Gill: 'him that appointed him' (or 'made him') — Christ as man was made, but not as God; the apostle is not speaking of the divine nature of Christ but of his offices, so this phrase designs the constitution and settlement of him in office, which may take in the eternal appointment of him; Paul declaring that Christ was faithful to him that appointed him, as also Moses was faithful in all his house — the faithfulness common to Christ and Moses as the ground of comparison (Paul, before showing Christ's superiority, first granting the point of likeness: Christ 'was faithful to him that appointed him,' perfectly discharged the office God set him in, faithful in all his trust, 'as also Moses was faithful in all his house,' for Moses too was a faithful servant, of whom God himself testified that he was faithful in all his house, Israel the church of that age, so the comparison begins on common ground, both faithful in their charge, that the greater glory of Christ may then appear).

themes: who was faithful to him that appointed him
as also Moses was faithful in all his house
the faithfulness common to Christ and Moses
the comparison beginning on common ground

schools.summary: Gill: Moses was counted worthy of glory and honour and had it given him, both by God and by men — by God in the work he called him to, to deliver his people Israel, to reveal his mind and will to them, and to rule and govern them; Paul declaring that Christ was counted worthy of more glory than Moses, as the builder of the house has more honour than the house — the superiority of Christ the builder over Moses who was but part of the house (Paul, having granted Moses his glory, now showing Christ's to be greater and proving it by a plain comparison: 'he who hath builded the house hath more honour than the house,' Moses, for all his honour, being himself only a part of the house, the people of God, a member of it, but Christ the builder and founder of that house, and as the architect is more honoured than the building he raises, so Christ the builder far surpasses Moses, who was but a stone in the building).

themes: for this man was counted worthy of more glory than Moses
he who hath builded the house hath more honour than the house
Christ the builder over Moses part of the house
the architect surpassing the building

schools.summary: Gill: every house is built by some one, for a house does not build itself — this is true of houses properly taken, or improperly, as nations, tribes, families, and kindred, of the whole church in general, of particular congregations, and of individual believers; Paul reasoning that every house is built by some man, but he that built all things is God — the builder of all is God, and Christ the builder is therefore God (Paul pressing his argument to its height with a great principle: 'every house is builded by some man,' every house must have a builder, 'but he that built all things is God,' the universal Builder, who made and frames all things, the whole creation and the whole church, being God himself; the force of this for Christ's deity: Christ has just been named the builder of God's house, and here the builder of all is declared to be God, so the One who builds the house, Christ, is no creature like Moses but God, the maker of all).

debates.question: What does 'he that built all things is God' imply about Christ?

sides.position: Since Christ has just been named the builder of God's house, and the builder of all things is God, the argument bears on Christ's deity — he who builds is no creature like Moses but God, the maker of all

themes: for every house is builded by some man
but he that built all things is God
the universal Builder is God
the argument bearing on Christ's deity

schools.summary: Gill: Moses was not only a servant to the Israelites but also the Lord's servant, a servant of his choosing, sending, and approving; he was a servant in holy things and served the Lord heartily, sincerely, and ingenuously, with all becoming faithfulness; Paul declaring that Moses was faithful in all his house, but as a servant, for a testimony of the things to be spoken after — the honourable but subordinate place of Moses as a servant pointing to Christ (Paul defining the precise nature of Moses' faithfulness, to set it below Christ's: Moses 'verily was faithful in all his house,' truly and fully faithful, an honourable thing, but 'as a servant,' in the rank and place of a servant in the house, not its lord, and even his ministry prophetic, 'for a testimony of those things which were to be spoken after,' all his service pointing forward, witnessing beforehand to the gospel and to Christ who was to come, so Moses' very honour was that of a faithful servant testifying to another greater than himself).

themes: and Moses verily was faithful in all his house, as a servant
for a testimony of those things which were to be spoken after
Moses faithful but only as a servant
his ministry prophetic, pointing to Christ

schools.summary: Gill: Christ is a Son, not by creation, or by adoption, or by office, but by nature, being the only begotten of the Father, having the same nature and perfections with him, and so Lord and master over his own house, which Moses was not (though the Jews say Moses was 'lord and master of the house,' yea 'the Son of the house,' but this he was not); Paul declaring that Christ is as a Son over his own house, whose house we are, if we hold fast the confidence and rejoicing of hope firm to the end — the lordship of Christ the Son over the house and the believer's perseverance (Paul crowning the comparison: while Moses served as a servant in the house, 'Christ as a son over his own house,' not a servant but the Son and Lord ruling over the house that is his own, and applying it: 'whose house are we,' believers being that very house of God in the gospel age, and adding the condition and evidence of belonging to it, 'if we hold fast the confidence and the rejoicing of the hope firm unto the end,' not as making salvation depend on us but as marking that the true members of Christ's house are known by their persevering confidence and hope to the end).

debates.question: Does 'if we hold fast... unto the end' make belonging to Christ's house conditional on us?

sides.position: Perseverance is the mark and evidence of the true members of Christ's house, not the ground of salvation — the genuine are known by holding fast confidence and hope to the end

themes: but Christ as a son over his own house
whose house are we
if we hold fast the confidence and the rejoicing of the hope firm unto the end
perseverance the mark of true members

schools.summary: Chrysostom: Paul, having treated of hope and said that 'we are his house, if we hold fast the confidence and the rejoicing of the hope firm unto the end,' next shows that we ought to look forward with firmness, and proves this from the Scriptures, expressly ascribing the words to the Holy Ghost; Paul introducing the Spirit's word, 'Today if ye will hear his voice,' as the ground of a solemn warning — the divine voice of the Spirit calling for present obedience (Paul, to enforce the perseverance just urged, turning to Scripture, Psalm 95, introducing the quotation with a weighty note of its authorship, 'as the Holy Ghost saith,' the words being the very voice of the Spirit of God, and the word he cites itself a present call, 'To day if ye will hear his voice,' a summons to hear and obey God's voice now in the present 'today' of opportunity; Paul's ascription of the Old Testament Scripture to the Holy Ghost as the divine Author, and the urgency of the 'today' that presses immediate obedience).

debates.question: Whose voice is the Old Testament Scripture here cited?

sides.position: The Holy Ghost's — Paul ascribes the words of Psalm 95 directly to the Holy Ghost as their divine Author ('as the Holy Ghost saith'), a witness to the Spirit's inspiration of Scripture

themes: wherefore (as the Holy Ghost saith
To day if ye will hear his voice
the Scripture the voice of the Holy Ghost
the 'today' pressing immediate obedience

schools.summary: Gill: there is a natural hardness of the heart — the heart of man is like a stone, destitute of spiritual life, motion, and activity, senseless, stupid, impenitent, stubborn, and inflexible, on which no impressions can be made but by powerful grace — and there is an acquired, habitual hardness; Paul citing the warning, 'Harden not your hearts, as in the provocation, in the day of temptation in the wilderness' — the call not to repeat Israel's hardening of heart against God (Paul continuing the Spirit's word from Psalm 95, applying Israel's history as a warning: 'harden not your hearts,' do not by unbelief and rebellion stiffen and steel your hearts against God's voice, 'as in the provocation, in the day of temptation in the wilderness,' as the fathers did when at Meribah and Massah they provoked God and put him to the test by their murmuring, so the present hearers are solemnly cautioned not to imitate that hardening which brought ruin on their fathers).

themes: harden not your hearts
as in the provocation, in the day of temptation in the wilderness
the warning against hardening the heart
not to imitate the fathers' rebellion

schools.summary: Gill: the apostle cites and repeats this to expose the glorying of the Jews in their ancestors, to dissuade them from following their sinful practices, to deter them from the same by observing both their sin and punishment, and to heighten their regards to the voice and gospel of God; Paul recalling that their fathers tempted God, proved him, and saw his works forty years — the aggravated sin of provoking God amid the sight of his wonders (Paul recounting the fathers' guilt: 'when your fathers tempted me, proved me,' put God to the trial as if doubting his power and faithfulness by their repeated unbelief and murmuring, and that with the deepest aggravation, 'and saw my works forty years,' for all the while they tempted him they were beholding his mighty works of judgment and mercy, the daily manna, the cloud, the deliverances, for forty long years, so their unbelief was the more inexcusable, sinning against the clearest and most prolonged evidence of God's presence and power).

themes: when your fathers tempted me, proved me
and saw my works forty years
the aggravated sin of tempting God amid his works
unbelief against the clearest evidence

schools.summary: Gill: 'that generation' is what the Jews often call 'the generation of the wilderness,' which they say was more beloved than any generation, yet they will not allow them a part in the world to come; God's being said to be 'grieved' with them is to be considered after the manner of men; Paul declaring that God was grieved with that generation, saying they always err in their heart and have not known his ways — the divine displeasure at a people incurably astray in heart (Paul giving God's own verdict on the provoking generation: 'I was grieved with that generation,' God displeased and offended at them, the language of grief used after the manner of men, and the ground of it: 'they do alway err in their heart,' their wandering not occasional but constant and inward, a habitual straying of the heart itself, 'and they have not known my ways,' they remaining ignorant of and indifferent to God's ways, refusing to learn them, so the root of their ruin was a settled, heart-deep estrangement from God).

themes: wherefore I was grieved with that generation
they do alway err in their heart
and they have not known my ways
a settled, heart-deep estrangement from God

schools.summary: Gill: swearing is ascribed to God to show the certainty of the thing spoken of — as of mercies, when he swears in love and by his holiness, so here of punishment, when he swears in wrath, in indignation, in sore displeasure, and the threatened evil is irrevocable and inevitable; Paul citing that God sware in his wrath that they should not enter into his rest — the irrevocable sentence of exclusion from God's rest (Paul giving the dreadful issue of the fathers' unbelief: 'so I sware in my wrath,' God, provoked at last, confirming his sentence with an oath, the most solemn and unchangeable form of his word, 'They shall not enter into my rest,' into the rest of the land of Canaan and, beyond it, the rest of God, so their unbelief shut them out by a sentence sealed with God's own oath, irreversible and certain, the carcasses of that generation falling in the wilderness, never to reach the promised rest).

themes: so I sware in my wrath
They shall not enter into my rest
the irrevocable sentence of exclusion
the oath sealing their exclusion from rest

schools.summary: Gill: this exhortation is grounded upon the state and case of their ancestors before given, as a warning and caution to the then present Hebrews; Paul styles them 'brethren' to show that he had no hard thoughts of them, and that his jealousy was a godly one and not an evil suspicion; Paul charging the brethren to take heed lest there be in any an evil heart of unbelief, departing from the living God — the solemn caution against the unbelief that leads to apostasy (Paul pressing the application of Israel's fall upon the present hearers: 'take heed, brethren,' watch carefully over yourselves, 'lest there be in any of you an evil heart of unbelief,' for unbelief is no light fault but 'an evil heart,' a root of wickedness, and naming where it leads, 'in departing from the living God,' the apostasy and falling away from the true and living God to which unbelief tends, so each is to watch his own heart, knowing that an unbelieving heart, if unchecked, ends in departure from God).

themes: take heed, brethren, lest there be in any of you an evil heart of unbelief
in departing from the living God
the caution against the unbelief that leads to apostasy
unbelief a root ending in departure from God

schools.summary: Gill: the phrase is sometimes rendered 'comfort one another,' or 'yourselves together,' which the saints may do by discoursing together about divine things, by praying together, and by instructing one another in the doctrines of the gospel, in order to prevent unbelief and apostasy; Paul charging that they exhort one another daily, while it is called Today, lest any be hardened through the deceitfulness of sin — the daily mutual exhortation that guards against sin's hardening power (Paul giving the preventive against the evil heart of unbelief: not solitary watchfulness only but the mutual care of the body, 'exhort one another daily,' admonish, encourage, and stir up one another every day in constant brotherly care, 'while it is called To day,' so long as the present season of opportunity lasts, and naming the danger this guards against, 'lest any of you be hardened through the deceitfulness of sin,' for sin is deceitful, hardening the heart by slow and unfelt degrees, and the daily exhortation of brethren is God's appointed means to keep any from being so deceived).

themes: but exhort one another daily, while it is called To day
lest any of you be hardened through the deceitfulness of sin
daily mutual exhortation against sin's hardening
sin hardening by slow, unfelt degrees

schools.summary: Gill: 'we are made partakers of Christ' — being loved by him, given to him, and chosen in him before the foundation of the world, and so participating of all spiritual blessings in him; this respects something past and may be rendered 'we have been made,' the phrase expressive of union to Christ; Paul declaring that we are made partakers of Christ, if we hold the beginning of our confidence steadfast to the end — the union with Christ evidenced by perseverance to the end (Paul giving a further motive to the steadfastness urged: 'we are made partakers of Christ,' sharers in him and all his benefits, united to him, a great and blessed thing, and adding the same evidence as before, 'if we hold the beginning of our confidence stedfast unto the end,' this 'if' not as the cause by which we become partakers but as the proof and sign of it, those truly united to Christ being known by holding their first confidence firm to the end, persevering faith being the evidence of a real share in Christ).

debates.question: Does 'if we hold... unto the end' make our partaking of Christ conditional?

sides.position: The 'if' is the proof and sign of a real share in Christ, not the cause by which we become partakers — those truly united to him persevere, holding their first confidence firm to the end

themes: for we are made partakers of Christ
if we hold the beginning of our confidence stedfast unto the end
union with Christ evidenced by perseverance
persevering faith the proof, not the cause

schools.summary: Gill: this repetition of the citation is in order to make a further improvement of it, which shows that the words belong to the present times of the gospel and contain in them matter of present concern; Paul repeating, 'Today if ye will hear his voice, harden not your hearts, as in the provocation' — the present urgency of the warning re-applied to the hearers (Paul, before drawing his conclusions, repeating the Spirit's word once more to drive it home: 'while it is said, To day if ye will hear his voice, harden not your hearts, as in the provocation,' the very fact that the Scripture still says 'Today' showing that the warning is not a dead letter of the past but a living word of present concern, pressing the gospel hearers, now, while their 'today' lasts, to hear God's voice and not harden their hearts as the fathers did, so the call is renewed with all its urgency upon the present generation).

themes: while it is said, To day if ye will hear his voice
harden not your hearts, as in the provocation
the present urgency of the re-applied warning
the 'Today' a living word, not a dead letter

schools.summary: Gill: 'when they had heard' — the law on Mount Sinai, the voice of words with the thunderings that attended it, the book of the covenant read, the whole system of laws and ordinances delivered to Moses and by him to them, and also the gospel, for that was preached to them in the wilderness; Paul observing that some who had heard did provoke, though not all who came out of Egypt by Moses — the provoking were those who heard, yet not without exception (Paul, by a series of questions, driving the lesson home, first noting who it was that provoked God: 'some, when they had heard, did provoke,' the very people who had heard God's voice and word being the ones who rebelled, so hearing alone does not save, yet 'howbeit not all that came out of Egypt by Moses,' for there were exceptions, Caleb and Joshua who believed, so the mass of those who heard fell by provoking unbelief, though a faithful few were preserved).

themes: for some, when they had heard, did provoke
howbeit not all that came out of Egypt by Moses
hearing alone does not save
the mass fell, a faithful few preserved

schools.summary: Barnes: 'with whom was he grieved forty years' — with whom was he angry? Was it not with them that had sinned, that had sinned in various ways, by rebellion, murmuring, and unbelief? As God was angry with them for their sins; Paul asking with whom God was grieved forty years — with them that had sinned, whose carcasses fell in the wilderness — the objects of God's grief were the sinners who perished in the desert (Paul putting the second question and answering it: 'with whom was he grieved forty years?' the answer plain, 'was it not with them that had sinned,' the rebels and murmurers and unbelievers, 'whose carcases fell in the wilderness,' who died under God's judgment, their dead bodies strewn across the desert, never reaching the land, so God's long displeasure fell upon the sinners, and the visible proof of it was that whole generation perishing in the wilderness without entering the rest).

themes: but with whom was he grieved forty years?
was it not with them that had sinned
whose carcases fell in the wilderness
God's displeasure falling on the sinners who perished

schools.summary: Barnes: 'to them that believed not' — that did not confide in God ('Yet in this thing ye did not believe the Lord your God,' Deuteronomy 1:32); in consequence of this want of faith, God solemnly sware they should not enter; Paul asking to whom God sware they should not enter his rest — to them that believed not — the exclusion from rest was the penalty of unbelief (Paul putting the third and climactic question, narrowing the cause of their fall to its root: 'to whom sware he that they should not enter into his rest?' the answer fixing the precise sin, 'but to them that believed not,' so that, while they had sinned in many ways, the one sin that shut them out of the rest, the sin against which God's oath was sworn, was unbelief, their refusal to trust God's word and promise, so unbelief is singled out as the deadly, excluding sin, the very thing the Hebrews are warned to shun).

themes: and to whom sware he that they should not enter into his rest
but to them that believed not
the exclusion from rest the penalty of unbelief
unbelief the deadly, excluding sin

schools.summary: Gill: they could not enter into God's rest, the land of Canaan, for they died by the plague before the Lord and their carcasses fell in the wilderness before they came to it, and the reason was their unbelief; Barnes: we see, from the direct testimony of the Old Testament, that unbelief was the cause; Paul concluding that they could not enter in because of unbelief — the plain lesson that unbelief is what shuts men out of God's rest (Paul gathering the whole argument into one solemn conclusion: 'so we see that they could not enter in because of unbelief,' the testimony of the Old Testament itself showing plainly that it was unbelief, and nothing else, that barred that generation from the promised rest, their carcasses falling in the wilderness; this the great practical point of the whole passage, that unbelief is the fatal, excluding sin, and the warning to the Hebrews and to all to beware of that same unbelief lest they too come short of God's rest).

themes: so we see that they could not enter in
because of unbelief
unbelief the fatal, excluding sin
the warning to beware of that same unbelief
